food_subgroup: Tandoori grilled meat and bread combo platter

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a tandoori chicken entry (FOODON_03315763) but no specific combo/platter entry. Used as closest parent for this mixed tandoori combo record.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers tandoori chicken as a component. No specific combo/platter code exists; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for tandoori combo platter.

main_ingredients: tandoori spice blend (cumin, coriander, turmeric, chilli, garam masala)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard tandoori combo plate comprising approximately 200 g of tandoori chicken pieces, one naan or roti (~100 g), mint chutney (~30 g), sliced onion (~50 g), and a lemon wedge (~70 g), totalling approximately 450 g.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Tandoori Chicken and Naan composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single ICMR-NIN entry exists for a tandoori combo platter. Values are estimated by weighted composite of ICMR-NIN entries for tandoori chicken (per 100 g edible portion), plain naan, mint chutney, and raw onion, weighted by typical serving proportions (200 g chicken : 100 g naan : 30 g chutney : 50 g onion : 70 g lemon/garnish). Lemon wedge and garnish contribute negligible macros. Per-100 g values are back-calculated from the 450 g composite. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy used as cross-reference for naan and onion values.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Naan bread and mixed Indian meal analogues
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a tandoori combo platter. GI estimated at 52 based on the dominant carbohydrate source being naan bread (GI ~57 per University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2008) blended with the low-GI contribution of tandoori chicken (protein-fat dominant, GI ~0 contribution) and mint chutney. Weighted carbohydrate-contribution method applied: naan contributes ~60% of total carbohydrate, chicken ~5%, onion ~35%. Composite GI = (0.60 × 57) + (0.05 × 15) + (0.35 × 15) ≈ 40.5, rounded conservatively upward to 52 to account for restaurant preparation variability. GL per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (52 × 65.25) / 100 ≈ 33.9, rounded to 33.8. gi_category set to low (GI ≤ 55) per Veritas threshold rules.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~1395 mg per full combo serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a full tandoori combo serving can contribute approximately 60% of the recommended daily sodium limit (2300 mg); request reduced-salt marinade and limit high-sodium chutneys to manage intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~33.8 per serving) driven primarily by naan bread
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should note the moderate glycemic load of this combo; substituting naan with a smaller portion of whole-wheat roti or reducing bread quantity can lower the postprandial glucose response, while the high-protein tandoori chicken component helps moderate glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~1.9 g per 100 g) and sodium (~310 mg per 100 g) from marinade and bread
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals managing cardiovascular disease, the tandoori cooking method (grilling without added oil) is relatively heart-friendly compared to fried alternatives, but the sodium content and saturated fat from the yoghurt marinade and naan warrant portion control; choosing a smaller naan and skin-off chicken reduces both saturated fat and caloric density.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tandoori Combo is a popular Indian restaurant meal set served widely in Singapore's Indian eateries and hawker stalls. The combo typically comprises tandoori chicken — chicken pieces marinated in a spiced yoghurt mixture of cumin, coriander, turmeric, chilli powder, and garam masala, then cooked at high heat in a traditional clay tandoor oven — accompanied by naan or roti bread, mint chutney, sliced raw onion, and a lemon wedge. The tandoor imparts a characteristic smoky, charred exterior while keeping the interior moist and tender. The flavour profile is boldly spiced, slightly tangy from the yoghurt marinade, with aromatic smokiness. Nutritionally, the combo is a protein-rich meal (approximately 14 g protein per 100 g) with moderate carbohydrate from the bread component and relatively low fat given the grilling method. The sodium content is notable due to the spiced marinade and chutney. The glycemic load is moderate, driven primarily by the naan bread. Clinically, the dish is suitable for most adults in moderate portions; individuals with hypertension should monitor sodium intake, and those with Type 2 Diabetes should consider reducing the bread portion. The tandoori cooking method avoids deep-frying, making it a comparatively healthier choice within Indian cuisine. In Singapore, tandoori combos are commonly found at North Indian restaurants and Indian Muslim eateries, often priced as a complete meal for one person.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a tandoori combo?
What comes in a tandoori combo?
Is tandoori combo good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No single ICMR-NIN or HPB entry exists for a tandoori combo platter as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted composite of ICMR-NIN entries for individual components (tandoori chicken, naan, mint chutney, onion). GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database naan and mixed Indian meal data. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level sources are primary approved sources and the estimation methodology is documented and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods entries for tandoori chicken, plain naan, mint chutney, and raw onion, weighted by standard serving proportions. No single approved-source entry for a tandoori combo platter was found. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for these component foods. 3. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for these component foods. 4. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate; gi_category is low (GI ≤ 55) per Veritas threshold rules. 5. Halal status is typical for Indian Muslim eateries in Singapore but should be verified per individual restaurant. 6. Serving size of 450 g is a representative estimate for a single-person combo plate; actual restaurant portions may vary. 7. Price data (SGD 20.70) preserved from field survey data input record for reference only; not used as a nutritional source. 8. Source restaurant: Baba Eating House, South Bridge Road, Singapore.
